claim: Among UK undergraduates, the top assessment-related GenAI uses in 2025 were explaining concepts, summarising articles, and suggesting research ideas — comprehension tasks outrank writing tasks.
evidence: N=1,041 full-time UK undergraduates (Savanta, fieldwork Dec 2024): 'explain concepts' 58% (up from 36% in 2024), summarising articles second most popular (~48%, up from ~a third in 2024), suggesting research ideas ~41%; one-quarter use AI-generated text to help draft assessments; 18% put AI-generated-and-edited text into assessed work; 88% used GenAI for assessments (up from 53%), 92% used AI in any way (up from 66%).
source: HEPI/Kortext, Student Generative AI Survey 2025 (Policy Note 61), https://www.hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2025/ (PDF: hepi.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/HEPI-Kortext-Student-Generative-AI-Survey-2025.pdf)

verdict.corrected_claim: Among UK undergraduates (HEPI/Kortext Student Generative AI Survey 2025, Policy Note 61; Savanta poll of 1,041 full-time UK undergraduates, Dec 2024), the top assessment-related GenAI uses were explaining concepts (58%, up from 36% in 2024), summarising a relevant article (48%, up from 24% per the report's Figure 2, though the report's prose says "up from a third"), and suggesting research ideas (41%, up from 25%). Comprehension/ideation tasks outranked writing tasks: 25% used AI-generated text to help draft assessments (i.e., used it in assessments after editing it), 18% used AI-generated text edited with AI in assessed work, and 8% used it without editing. 88% used GenAI for assessments (up from 53%); 92% used AI in some form (up from 66%).
verdict.notes: Located and fetched the primary source: the HEPI landing page (hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2025/) and the full PDF (HEPI-Kortext-Student-Generative-AI-Survey-2025.pdf), which I downloaded, text-extracted with pdftotext, and rendered Figure 2 as an image with PyMuPDF to read the chart values directly. Confirmed: (a) it is HEPI Policy Note 61, February 2025, by Josh Freeman; (b) methodology: "we polled 1,041 full-time undergraduate students through Savanta in December 2024," weighted, ~3% margin of error; (c) prose verbatim: "To 'explain concepts' remains the most popular function, with 58% of students using it for this purpose, up from 36% in 2024... the use of AI to summarise articles... is now the second most popular use of GenAI, up from a third in 2024. One-quarter of students use AI-generated text to help them draft assessments and nearly a fifth of students (18%) use AI-generated and edited text in their assessments"; (d) Figure 2 chart values match the claim: summarise a relevant article 48% (second), suggest research ideas 41% (third); (e) 88% used GenAI for assessments vs 53% in 2024; 92% used AI in some form vs 66% in 2024. The ranking supports the interpretation: the top three uses (explain 58%, summarise 48%, research ideas 41%, plus structure thoughts 39%) all outrank the writing/text-insertion uses (25%, 18%, 8%). Two minor caveats: (1) the source contains an internal inconsistency — the prose says summarising is "up from a third in 2024" while its own Figure 2 shows 24% (about a quarter); the claim's "up from ~a third" faithfully echoes the prose but the chart figure is 24%; (2) "suggesting research ideas" is ideation rather than strictly comprehension, but this does not change the comprehension-over-writing ordering. Every number, the year, the population, and the interpretation check out against the primary source.

claim: By the 2026 HEPI wave, GenAI use for assessed work is near-universal among UK undergraduates and the task ranking is stable: explaining concepts > summarising articles > research ideas ≈ structuring thoughts > AI web search > drafting; direct pasting of AI text remains a small minority.
evidence: N=1,054 full-time UK undergraduates (Savanta, Dec 2025): 94% use GenAI for assessed work (53% in 2024, 88-89% in 2025); explain concepts 61%, summarise a relevant article ~49% ('half'), suggest ideas for research 40%, structure my thoughts 39%, use AI to search the internet 36% (new option), generate text then self-edit ~25%, include AI-generated text directly 12% (vs 8% in 2025, 3% in 2024); 95% use AI in at least one way.
source: HEPI/Kortext, Student Generative AI Survey 2026 (Report 199), https://www.hepi.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/HEPI-Report-199-Gen-AI-Survey-2026.pdf

verdict.corrected_claim: By the 2026 HEPI/Kortext wave (Report 199, N=1,054 full-time UK undergraduates polled via Savanta in Dec 2025), GenAI use for assessed work is near-universal (94%, up from ~53% in 2024 and 89% in 2025 on the comparable series; the 2025 report itself reported 88%) and the task ranking is stable: explaining concepts 61% > summarising a relevant article 49% > suggesting research ideas 40% ≈ structuring thoughts 39% > AI web search 36% (new option) > generating text then self-editing 25%. Including AI-generated text directly remains a minority at 12%, but this is a significant rise (8% in 2025, 3% in 2024). 95% use AI in at least one way (any purpose, not only assessed work).
verdict.notes: Located and fetched the exact primary source: HEPI Report 199, Student Generative AI Survey 2026 (hepi.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/HEPI-Report-199-Gen-AI-Survey-2026.pdf, 52 pp.), plus the HEPI report landing page (hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2026/). Extracted the PDF text directly and checked every number. Methodology section confirms: 1,054 full-time undergraduate students polled through Savanta in December 2025 (UCAS did the 2024 wave), margin of error ~3%. Prose confirms: 94% use generative AI to help with assessed work ("just over half" = 53% in 2024, 89% in 2025 as restated in this report; the 2025 report originally said 88%, so the claim's "88-89%" is fair); 95% use AI in at least one way (66% in 2024, 92% in 2025 — note this is AI for ANY purpose, not just assessed work, as the evidence correctly implies). Figure 4 (2026 column, verified from extracted chart data and prose): explain concepts 61%, summarise a relevant article 49% (prose: "half"), suggest ideas for research 40%, structure my thoughts 39%, use AI to search the internet 36% (new option, asterisked), generate text then edited before incorporating 25%, include AI-generated text directly 12% (prose: "increased from 8% of students in 2025 and 3% of students in 2024"). Ranking matches the claim and is stable vs 2025 (58/47/40/39/25). Report is dated/published March 2026 per URL path and search results. Only nuances: (1) the 12% direct-inclusion figure, while still a small minority, is a flagged significant increase (quadrupled since 2024), which the claim's evidence acknowledges; (2) 2025 assessed-work baseline is 89% in this report's comparable series vs 88% in the 2025 publication. No refuting discrepancies found. Sources: https://www.hepi.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/HEPI-Report-199-Gen-AI-Survey-2026.pdf and https://www.hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2026/

claim: For general (non-assessment) use, text generation is the single most popular AI activity among UK students but is declining, while textbook summarising/note-taking/quizzing and writing-editing tools form the second tier.
evidence: HEPI 2026 (N=1,054): generating text e.g. ChatGPT 56% (down from 64% in 2025, 30% in 2024); summarising/note-taking/quizzing university textbooks (e.g. Kortext) is the second most popular use; enhancing/editing writing (e.g. Grammarly) 38%; translation/language support ~37%; writing code ~20%; speech-to-text 18%; data analysis ~15%.
source: HEPI/Kortext, Student Generative AI Survey 2026 (Report 199), https://www.hepi.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/HEPI-Report-199-Gen-AI-Survey-2026.pdf

verdict.corrected_claim: For general (any-purpose) use, text generation (e.g. ChatGPT) is the single most popular AI activity among UK students but fell from 64% (2025) to 56% (2026), having been 30% in 2024. Summarising/note-taking/quizzing university textbooks (e.g. Kortext, 38%) is the second most popular use, followed by enhancing/editing writing (e.g. Grammarly, 37%). Other 2026 figures: translation/language support 31%, generating images/video/audio 22%, speech-to-text 18%, writing code 17%, data analysis and presentation 17%. (HEPI/Kortext Student Generative AI Survey 2026, Report 199, N=1,054 full-time UK undergraduates, Savanta fieldwork December 2025.)
verdict.notes: Fetched the primary source PDF at the exact URL given (HEPI Report 199, Student Generative AI Survey 2026, 52 pages) and extracted Figure 1 (p.11) with positional PDF parsing to map each percentage to its bar and year. VERIFIED: N=1,054 full-time UK undergraduates, polled via Savanta December 2025 (p.8); text generation 56% in 2026, down from 64% in 2025, 30% in 2024 — exact match; report text explicitly states generating text "remains the most popular use of AI" despite the drop, with summarising/note-taking/quizzing textbooks (Kortext) the "second most popular use... followed by enhancing or editing writing using tools such as Grammarly" — so the claim's ranking and trend interpretation are correct. IMPRECISIONS in the reported evidence: Grammarly is 37% not 38% (38% is the Kortext summarising option); translation/language support is 31% not ~37% (35% was the 2025 figure); writing code is 17% not ~20%; data analysis is 17% not ~15% (15% was the 2025 value); speech-to-text 18% is correct. One framing caveat: the question covers AI used "for any purpose, including your studies, employment, hobbies and so on" — it is the general-use question (distinct from the separate assessed-work question), not strictly "non-assessment" use. Also note "declining" applies only to the 2025-to-2026 change; the 2026 figure is still far above 2024's 30%. Core claim directionally and substantively correct, so verified=true with corrected figures supplied.

claim: Globally, information search is the #1 student AI use case, with writing-mechanics and summarising mid-tier and full drafting last.
evidence: N=3,839 students (bachelor/master/doctorate) across 16 countries, July 2024: search for information 69%, check grammar 42%, summarise documents 33%, paraphrase a document 28%, create a first draft 24%; 86% use AI in their studies, 54% at least weekly, 24% daily; ChatGPT used by 66%, Grammarly and Copilot 25% each; students use avg 2.1 AI tools.
source: Digital Education Council, Global AI Student Survey 2024, https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65f1d299b87bcc50550a6398/66a46f7b2a6137a25d3f69bb_Digital%20Education%20Council%20Global%20AI%20Student%20Survey%202024.pdf

claim: In the largest multi-country undergraduate survey, most GenAI-using students frame their use as comprehension support ('understanding subjects') rather than output generation.
evidence: N=11,706 undergraduates across 15 countries (Yonder Consulting, fieldwork Oct 1-23, 2024): 80% have used GenAI to support studies; 56% of GenAI users primarily rely on it to better understand subjects; 55% cite faster learning; 35% personalized learning; self-reported improvements: understanding complex concepts 50%, completing assignments 49%, organizing workload 41%.
source: Chegg.org, Global Student Survey 2025, https://www.chegg.org/global-student-survey-2025

claim: Within writing-related GenAI use, students mostly use it for mechanics (grammar) rather than full essay generation.
evidence: Chegg Global Student Survey 2025 (N=11,706 undergrads, 15 countries): among writing-related activities, grammar checking 58% of students, writing essays/papers 42%, summarizing long texts 39%; 53% of GenAI users concerned about inaccurate information.
source: Chegg press release via Businesswire/Nasdaq, 'Chegg Global Student Survey 2025: 80% of Undergraduates Worldwide Have Used GenAI', https://www.chegg.com/about/newsroom/press-release/chegg-global-student-survey-2025-80-of-undergraduates-worldwide-have-used-genai-to-support-their-studies-but-accuracy-a-top-concern

claim: In observed (not self-reported) usage of Claude.ai, students' top activities are creating/improving educational content and getting technical explanations/solutions, with the AI performing predominantly higher-order cognitive work.
evidence: ~1M anonymized Claude.ai conversations from higher-ed email accounts, 574,740 filtered as academic: create/improve educational content 39.3%, technical explanations/solutions 33.5%, data analysis/visualization 11.0%, research design/tool development 6.5%, technical diagrams 3.2%, translation/proofreading 2.4%; Bloom's taxonomy of AI's action: Creating 39.8%, Analyzing 30.2%, Applying 10.9%, Understanding 10.0%, Remembering 1.8%; ~47% of conversations were 'Direct' (answer-seeking) vs collaborative; CS students 38.6% of conversations vs 5.4% of US degrees.
source: Anthropic, 'Anthropic Education Report: How University Students Use Claude', https://www.anthropic.com/news/anthropic-education-report-how-university-students-use-claude

claim: OpenAI's usage data shows over a third of US college-aged adults use ChatGPT, with roughly a quarter of their messages learning-related, and the top uses being paper-starting, summarizing, brainstorming, and topic exploration.
evidence: OpenAI internal user-data analysis (US, 18-24): >1/3 use ChatGPT; ~25% of their messages relate to learning/tutoring/schoolwork; >40% of current users used it for starting papers and projects, summarizing texts, brainstorming creative projects, and exploring topics; math problem-solving, exam prep, and academic research also common (percentages not published); 3 in 4 college-aged users want AI in education/careers.
source: OpenAI, 'College students and ChatGPT adoption in the US' (openai.com/global-affairs/college-students-and-chatgpt/; primary page blocked 403, figures confirmed via Fortune https://fortune.com/education/articles/openai-report-more-than-one-third-college-aged-adults-use-chatgpt/ and BestColleges https://www.bestcolleges.com/news/students-embrace-chatgpt/)

claim: Among US teens (the incoming university cohort), schoolwork help is the #2 chatbot use after information search, and within schoolwork the top tasks are researching topics, solving math problems, and editing written work.
evidence: N=1,458 US teens 13-17 (fieldwork Sep 25-Oct 9, 2025, MoE ±3.3pp): 57% use chatbots to search for information, 54% for schoolwork help, ~40% to summarize articles/books/videos; within schoolwork: ~40%+ researching topics, ~40%+ solving math problems, 35% editing written work; 10% do all/most schoolwork with chatbot help, 21% some; 59% believe schoolmates use AI to cheat at least somewhat often.
source: Pew Research Center, 'How Teens Use and View AI', https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2026/02/24/how-teens-use-and-view-ai/

claim: US college students' regular GenAI use keeps rising, but reliance on AI as first resort when stuck on a concept collapsed after a 2024 spike — students still overwhelmingly turn to people first.
evidence: Time for Class 2025 (Spring 2025; student n=1,529 for usage question, n=1,210 for help-seeking; plus 1,500+ instructors, 300+ administrators at 900+ US institutions): 42% of students use GenAI weekly or daily (up from 36% in 2024, 15% in 2023); when struggling with a concept, students primarily turn to people 84%, static resources 58%, GenAI 17% — down from 30% in Spring 2024 (4% in 2023); ChatGPT used by 65% of GenAI-trying students vs Gemini 25%.
source: Tyton Partners, 'Time for Class 2025: Empowering Educators, Engaging Students', https://4213961.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hubfs/4213961/Publications/Time%20for%20Class/Tyton%20Partners_Time%20for%20Class%202025.pdf

claim: A significant minority of AI-using US college students have GenAI write large parts of assignments, exceeding what faculty say they permit.
evidence: Time for Class 2024 (Spring 2024; ~1,600 students, ~1,800 instructors): 59% of students used GenAI for schoolwork at least monthly (up from 43% in spring 2023); 27% of AI-using students report using AI to write large parts of assignments, and nearly half of AI-using students use tools to write content; ~20% of AI-using faculty allow first drafts/parts of assignments; 50% of students would keep using GenAI even if banned.
source: Tyton Partners, 'Time for Class 2024' via https://tytonpartners.com/racing-forward-bridging-the-gap-between-generative-ai-proficiency-and-educational-practice/ and Inside Higher Ed https://www.insidehighered.com/news/student-success/academic-life/2025/06/11/65-percent-students-use-gen-ai-chat-bot-weekly

claim: The largest academic survey of an entire national student population found 'clarifying questions of understanding / explaining subject-specific concepts' is the single most prevalent AI use case, roughly double any other category.
evidence: N=6,311 German university students (fieldwork May 15-Jun 5, 2023): 63.4% had used AI-based tools for their studies (49% explicitly ChatGPT/GPT-4); use cases among all surveyed: clarifying understanding/explaining concepts 56.5%, research and literature work 28.6%, translations 26.6%, text analysis/processing/creation 24.8%, problem-solving and decision-making 22.1%; engineering/math/natural-science students use AI most.
source: von Garrel, J. & Mayer, J., 'Artificial Intelligence in studies—use of ChatGPT and AI-based tools among students in Germany', Humanities and Social Sciences Communications 10:799, https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-023-02304-7

claim: Students' stated motivations for using GenAI are time-saving and quality improvement, while the main deterrents are fear of misconduct accusations and hallucinations — implying an assistant must be perceived as legitimate and accurate, not just capable.
evidence: HEPI 2025 (N=1,041 UK undergrads): 51% use AI mainly to save time, 50% to improve work quality; deterrents: 53% fear being accused of cheating, 51% fear false results/hallucinations; 76% say their institution would spot AI in assessed work; only 36% received institutional support to develop AI skills.
source: HEPI/Kortext, Student Generative AI Survey 2025, https://www.hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2025/

claim: Explicit exam-prep/self-quizzing is rarely broken out as its own category in student surveys and, where visible, sits below explanation and summarising — e.g., textbook quizzing/note-taking tools reach ~30% and OpenAI lists exam prep below its >40% top-four uses.
evidence: HEPI 2026: 'summarising, note-taking or quizzing university textbooks' is the #2 general use (~30% vs 56% text generation); OpenAI college report lists exam prep and math among 'other common' uses below the >40% tier (starting papers, summarizing, brainstorming, exploring topics); von Garrel & Mayer list exam preparation as a named but unquantified minor category; DEC 2024 has no exam-prep category at all.
source: Synthesis across HEPI Report 199 (hepi.ac.uk), OpenAI college students report (via Fortune/BestColleges), von Garrel & Mayer 2023 (nature.com), DEC Global AI Student Survey 2024 (digitaleducationcouncil.com)

claim: Planning/organizing studies is a secondary but real use: structuring thoughts is used by ~4 in 10 UK students, and organizing workload is one of the top three self-reported benefits globally.
evidence: HEPI 2026: 'used AI to structure my thoughts' 39% (36% in 2025); HEPI 2025 shows structuring thoughts at 44% among higher socio-economic (ABC1) students vs lower among C2DE; Chegg 2025 (N=11,706): 41% report improved workload organization from GenAI; Pew 2026 teens: ~20% of teen chatbot users get news, 16% casual conversation — organizational uses not separately tracked for teens.
source: HEPI Report 199 (hepi.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/HEPI-Report-199-Gen-AI-Survey-2026.pdf); Chegg.org Global Student Survey 2025 (chegg.org/global-student-survey-2025)

claim: Roughly one in seven UK students now uses AI for companionship or emotional support, a nascent use category adjacent to academic assistance.
evidence: HEPI 2026 (N=1,054): 15% use AI for friendship, company, advice or tackling loneliness; 8% use counselling/therapy provided solely by AI; 20% say AI makes them lonelier vs 21% less lonely; Pew 2026 (N=1,458 teens): 12% of teens use chatbots for emotional support or advice.
source: HEPI Report 199 (hepi.ac.uk); Pew Research Center 'How Teens Use and View AI' (pewresearch.org/internet/2026/02/24/how-teens-use-and-view-ai/)

claim: Student Claude usage is radically skewed toward Computer Science and STEM, while business, humanities, and health students are heavily underrepresented relative to enrollment.
evidence: From 574,740 anonymized student conversations (filtered from ~1M Claude.ai conversations from higher-ed email accounts): Computer Science = 38.6% of conversations vs 5.4% of US bachelor's degrees; Natural Sciences & Mathematics = 15.2% vs 9.2%; Business = 8.9% vs 18.6%; Humanities = 6.4% vs 12.5%; Health Professions = 5.5% vs 13.1%.
source: Anthropic, "Anthropic Education Report: How University Students Use Claude" — https://www.anthropic.com/news/anthropic-education-report-how-university-students-use-claude

verdict.notes: Fetched the primary source (anthropic.com/news/anthropic-education-report-how-university-students-use-claude, published Apr 8, 2025) three times with independent prompts, plus a corroborating web search. All reported figures match verbatim: 574,740 conversations filtered from ~1M anonymized Claude.ai Free/Pro conversations tied to higher-ed email addresses; CS 38.6% of conversations vs 5.4% of US bachelor's degrees; Natural Sciences & Math 15.2% vs 9.2%; Business 8.9% vs 18.6%; Humanities 6.4% vs 12.5%; Health Professions 5.5% vs 13.1% (degree shares via NCES). Interpretation ("radically skewed toward CS/STEM, business/humanities/health underrepresented relative to enrollment") matches the report's own framing. Minor caveats that do not falsify the claim: (a) the article's summary bullet once says CS = 36.8% of students' conversations while the detailed section says 38.6% — the claim matches the detailed figure; (b) the report notes the sample likely captures early adopters within a single 18-day retention window, and percentages are conversation-topic shares, not a census of student majors. Third-party coverage (Tibor Blaho on X, GIGAZINE) independently repeats 574,740 and 38.6%/5.4%.
verdict.corrected_claim: Accurate as stated. Optional precision: percentages are shares of Claude.ai conversations mapped to subject areas (from early-adopter students with higher-ed email addresses in an 18-day window), compared against NCES US bachelor's degree shares; the report also cites 36.8% in one summary sentence for CS, with 38.6% in the detailed breakdown.

claim: How students interact with AI differs by discipline: STEM students lean collaborative/problem-solving, while humanities, business, and health students split more evenly between collaborative and direct answer-seeking; nearly half of all student conversations are 'Direct' (minimal engagement).
evidence: Same 574,740-conversation dataset: four interaction patterns (Direct/Collaborative x Problem Solving/Output Creation) each ~23-29%; ~47% of conversations were Direct; top task categories were creating/improving educational content (39.3%) and technical explanations or solutions (33.5%), then data analysis/visualization (11.0%), research design (6.5%), technical diagrams (3.2%), translation/proofreading (2.4%); Natural Sciences/Math emphasized step-by-step problem solving, CS/Engineering favored collaborative debugging, Education showed strongest output creation (74.4%).
source: Anthropic, "Anthropic Education Report: How University Students Use Claude" — https://www.anthropic.com/news/anthropic-education-report-how-university-students-use-claude

verdict.notes: Located the primary source at the exact URL given: Anthropic, "Anthropic Education Report: How University Students Use Claude," published April 8, 2025. Ran three targeted fetches (summary pass, verbatim-quote pass, keyword pass) against the live page. Findings: (1) Dataset matches — ~1 million anonymized Claude.ai conversations from Free and Pro accounts tied to higher-education email addresses, filtered to "574,740 conversations" over an 18-day period. (2) Verbatim: "These four interaction styles were represented at similar rates (each between 23% and 29% of conversations)" — matches the ~23-29% figure. (3) Verbatim: "Nearly half (~47%) of student-AI conversations were Direct—that is, seeking answers or content with minimal engagement" — the ~47% Direct figure is explicit, not inferred. (4) Task-category percentages all match: create/improve educational content 39.3%, technical explanations or solutions 33.5%, analyze/visualize data 11.0%, research design/tool development 6.5%, technical diagrams 3.2%, translate/proofread 2.4%. (5) Discipline findings match: Natural Sciences & Mathematics "tended toward Problem Solving" with "step-by-step" examples; "Computer Science, Engineering, and Natural Sciences & Mathematics leaned towards Collaborative conversations"; "Humanities, Business, and Health were more evenly split... between Collaborative and Direct conversations"; "Education showed the strongest preference for Output Creation, covering 74.4% of conversations." Two minor imprecisions in the EVIDENCE AS REPORTED (not the claim itself): (a) "CS/Engineering favored collaborative debugging" conflates two separate statements — the report says CS/Engineering leaned Collaborative (Direct-vs-Collaborative axis), while "debug and fix errors in coding assignments" appears as an example under the technical-explanations task category (33.5%), not as a named "collaborative debugging" pattern; (b) the Problem Solving lean is stated specifically for Natural Sciences & Mathematics, not for STEM broadly. The headline claim is directionally and numerically accurate.
verdict.corrected_claim: Per Anthropic's Education Report (April 8, 2025; 574,740 filtered student conversations on Claude.ai over an 18-day period): interaction styles differ by discipline — Computer Science, Engineering, and Natural Sciences & Mathematics leaned toward Collaborative conversations (with Natural Sciences & Mathematics specifically tending toward Problem Solving), while Humanities, Business, and Health conversations were more evenly split between Collaborative and Direct; nearly half (~47%) of all student-AI conversations were Direct (seeking answers or content with minimal engagement), and the four interaction styles (Direct/Collaborative x Problem Solving/Output Creation) each accounted for 23-29% of conversations.

claim: In the UK, AI use among undergraduates is near-universal, with a documented digital divide favoring male, STEM/Health, and socioeconomically advantaged students.
evidence: N=1,041 full-time UK undergraduates (Savanta, Dec 2024, ~3% margin of error): 92% use AI (up from 66% in 2024), 88% used generative AI for assessments (up from 53%); report explicitly identifies higher use among male students, students on STEM and Health courses, and more advantaged students; motivations: saving time (51%), improving quality (50%); barriers: fear of cheating accusations (53%), hallucinations (51%).
source: Higher Education Policy Institute (HEPI)/Kortext, Josh Freeman, "Student Generative AI Survey 2025" — https://www.hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2025/

verdict.notes: Fetched the attributed primary source directly (hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2025/) and corroborated via web search (HEPI press release, full PDF, University World News). Every element of the evidence matches the source verbatim or near-verbatim: (1) Methodology: 1,041 online interviews with full-time undergraduate students conducted by Savanta in December 2024; page states 'The margin of error is approximately 3%.' The report is by Josh Freeman for HEPI/Kortext, published 26 February 2025, and concerns UK undergraduates. (2) '92% use AI (up from 66%)' — source: 'The proportion of students reporting using any AI tool has jumped from 66% last year to 92% this year.' The prior figure comes from the 2024 edition of the same survey, so 'up from 66% in 2024' is correct. (3) '88% used generative AI for assessments (up from 53%)' — source: 'using generative AI tools such as ChatGPT for assessments has jumped from 53% last year to 88% this year.' (4) Digital divide — source explicitly states: 'There is a growing digital divide in AI use, with male students, students on STEM and Health courses and more socioeconomically advantaged students more likely to use AI than others.' This directly supports the claim's demographic interpretation. (5) Motivations — save time 51%, improve quality of work 50%: exact match. (6) Barriers — being accused of cheating 53%, false results/'hallucinations' 51%: exact match. Interpretation checks: 'near-universal' is a fair characterization of 92% any-AI use; note the digital divide finding is about likelihood of use (relative differences), which the claim states correctly ('favoring' = higher use among those groups). No discrepancies in number, year, population, or interpretation found.

claim: By late 2025 UK undergraduate AI use plateaued at near-total saturation and AI-generated text is increasingly submitted in assessed work, with Arts & Humanities students feeling most under-supported in AI skills.
evidence: N=1,054 full-time UK undergraduates (Savanta, Dec 2025): 95% use AI in at least one way, 94% use generative AI for assessed work; share including AI-generated text in assessed work rose 3% (2024) → 8% (2025) → 12% (2026); ~15% use AI for companionship/loneliness; Arts and Humanities students particularly likely to feel under-supported.
source: Higher Education Policy Institute (HEPI), "Student Generative Artificial Intelligence Survey 2026" — https://www.hepi.ac.uk/reports/student-generative-ai-survey-2026/

claim: Large German national survey complicates the 'humanities lag' narrative: arts/humanities students' AI usage nearly matches engineering, while law/economics/social sciences and medicine/health trail substantially — but purposes differ sharply by field.
evidence: N=6,311 German students (May 15-Jun 5, 2023): overall 63.4% used AI tools for studies; Engineering 75.3%, Arts & Humanities 73.4%, Math & Natural Sciences 71.9%, Law/Economics/Social Sciences 58.4%, Medicine/Health 52.7%, Agriculture/Forestry/Nutrition 47.6%. Top use across fields: clarifying understanding/explaining concepts (56.5% of users); engineers add programming/simulations (27.2%) and problem-solving (30.3%); humanities students add literature research (30.3%), translation (28.6%), text analysis/creation (25.4%).
source: von Garrel & Mayer, "Artificial Intelligence in studies—use of ChatGPT and AI-based tools among students in Germany", Humanities and Social Sciences Communications 10:799 — https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-023-02304-7

claim: At a selective US liberal arts college, adoption gaps by discipline are extreme within one campus: natural-science majors near-universal adopters while literature and language majors lag by 30-40 points.
evidence: N=634 Middlebury College undergraduates (22.9% response rate, pop. 2,760): 82.5% used AI academically by Fall 2024 (from <10% before Spring 2023); by discipline: Natural Sciences 91.1%, Social Sciences 84.6%, Languages 57.4%, Literature 48.6%; gender gap 88.7% (male) vs 78.4% (female); below-median-GPA students 87.1% vs above-median 80.3%.
source: Contractor & Reyes, "Generative AI in Higher Education: Evidence from an Elite College", arXiv:2508.00717 — https://arxiv.org/html/2508.00717v1

claim: Students use AI more to augment learning than to automate output, and use-case frequencies quantify this: explanation and summarization dominate over essay-writing and coding.
evidence: Middlebury sample (N=634): among AI users — explaining concepts 80.3%, summarizing texts 74.0%, finding information 63.1%, generating ideas 61.9%, proofreading 54.1%, editing essays 47.3%, coding assistance 34.4%, writing essays 23.5%; augmentation users 61.2% vs automation users 41.9%; only 11.3% pay for any AI service; 89.9% unaware of free premium tools; 67.4% don't know how to cite AI properly.
source: Contractor & Reyes, "Generative AI in Higher Education: Evidence from an Elite College", arXiv:2508.00717 — https://arxiv.org/html/2508.00717v1

claim: Institutional AI policy strongly shapes stated student usage, implying an academic assistant must adapt to per-course policy contexts.
evidence: Middlebury sample: likelihood of using AI drops from 52.4% (unrestricted) to 40.9% (citation required) to 13.4% (explicitly prohibited) — a 37.8 pp decline under prohibition; 79.1% say they understand when/where AI is allowed.
source: Contractor & Reyes, "Generative AI in Higher Education: Evidence from an Elite College", arXiv:2508.00717 — https://arxiv.org/html/2508.00717v1

claim: Globally, 86% of students across 16 countries use AI in their studies, but universities are failing to meet their AI expectations, and information search — not writing — is the top use case.
evidence: N=3,839 bachelor's/master's/doctorate students, 16 countries: 86% use AI in studies, 24% daily, 54% at least weekly; ChatGPT 66%, Grammarly 25%, Copilot 25%; avg 2.1 tools/student; use cases: information searching 69%, grammar checking 42%, summarizing 33%, paraphrasing 28%, first drafts 24%; 58% feel they lack sufficient AI knowledge/skills, 48% feel unprepared for AI-enabled workplace, 80% say university AI integration does not fully meet expectations; 72-73% want AI training for students and faculty.
source: Digital Education Council, "Global AI Student Survey 2024: AI or Not AI - What Students Want" (full PDF) — https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65f1d299b87bcc50550a6398/66a46f7b2a6137a25d3f69bb_Digital%20Education%20Council%20Global%20AI%20Student%20Survey%202024.pdf

claim: Romanian student data: ChatGPT adoption in Bucharest is high (85.5% tried it) across 11 faculties spanning psychology, business, engineering, and medicine, but attitudes skew cautious-to-distrustful rather than enthusiastic.
evidence: N=508 Bucharest bachelor's and master's students (11 faculties incl. Psychology n=67, Automatics & Computers n=67, Medicine n=64, Engineering n=54, Business/Administration n=42; ages 20-49, M=33.7): 85.53% used ChatGPT at least once; attitude clusters: cautious 42.45% of sample, distrustful/fearful 29.67%, positive/open 27.86%; openness correlated with extraversion (r=0.56) and autonomy (r=0.64).
source: "An Analysis of Students' Attitudes Toward Artificial Intelligence—ChatGPT, in Particular", Behavioral Sciences 15(9):1179, DOI 10.3390/bs15091179 — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12466694/

claim: Romanian CS students adopted generative AI for coursework early: at POLITEHNICA University of Bucharest over 40% reported using generative AI for homework and lab assignments by late 2023.
evidence: Survey of 181 students (mostly 2nd/3rd year) in a Functional Programming course at POLITEHNICA University of Bucharest: >40% used generative AI for homework and labs; 31% became aware of code-generation tools within the prior year.
source: "ChatGPT in the classroom. Exploring its potential and limitations in a Functional Programming course", arXiv:2401.11166 — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2401.11166

claim: Health-discipline students adopt AI later and use it heavily for information lookup and translation rather than problem-solving: about two-thirds of Chinese medical students had used ChatGPT, with very low daily use.
evidence: N=1,133 medical students, Sichuan Province, China (surveyed May-Nov 2024): 62.9% had used ChatGPT in their medical studies; purposes: information searching 84.4%, academic assignments 60.4%, translation 59.0%, research ideas 45.9%, polishing papers 41.4%; only 8.1% daily users; top concerns: misinformation 76.9%, plagiarism 65.4%.
source: "Status and perceptions of ChatGPT utilization among medical students: a survey-based study", BMC Medical Education — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12135314/

claim: US law students were early experimenters relative to lawyers and overwhelmingly expect AI to transform legal practice, though this predates modern legal AI tools.
evidence: N=1,239 US law students (of 4,180 total respondents, surveyed March 2023): 44% had used generative AI personally or professionally (vs 36% of lawyers, 32% of consumers); 92% of law students think generative AI will have at least some impact on the practice of law; 46% see the impact as 'significant' or 'transformative' (vs 39% of lawyers).
source: LexisNexis Legal & Professional, "Generative AI & the Legal Profession: 2023 Survey Report" — https://www.lexisnexis.com/pdf/ln_generative_ai_report.pdf

claim: Research postgraduates use generative AI more frequently than undergraduates and taught postgraduates, consistent with independent research workflows benefiting most from AI support.
evidence: Survey of 680 students at Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Hong Kong + Guangzhou campuses): research postgraduate students reported more frequent ChatGPT use and stronger continuance intention than undergraduate and taught-postgraduate peers; significant variation by gender, academic level, age, discipline, and geographic origin.
source: "Embracing generative artificial intelligence tools in higher education: a survey study at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology", Journal of Asian Public Policy, DOI 10.1080/17516234.2024.2447195 — https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/17516234.2024.2447195

claim: Central/Eastern European student data exists but is sparse and skewed toward IT students; students there report high AI-application confidence with moderate anxiety, and an open dataset covering Slovakia, Poland, Czech Republic, Lithuania, and Ukraine is available for secondary analysis.
evidence: N=1,146 unique students (1,205 responses, 2022-2024): Slovakia 56.9%, Poland 24.8%, Czech Republic 8.8%, Lithuania 3.2%, Indonesia 5.4%, plus Turkey/France/Ukraine; 69.1% IT students, 15.6% education majors; strong negative skew on AI literacy items (high confidence), moderate AI anxiety, relatively strong career motivation; open data: Figshare DOI 10.6084/m9.figshare.29488523.v2.
source: "Cross-national survey data on student attitudes toward artificial intelligence" (Data in Brief) — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12545832/

claim: Engineering-school AI adoption rose significantly between 2023 and 2024, driven by comprehension and quality goals rather than answer automation, but the student body is polarized about AI's societal impact.
evidence: Two survey waves at Colorado School of Mines (engineering-focused R1): n=601 (May 2023) and n=862 (Sep 2024); statistically significant rise in GenAI adoption; top uses: deepening understanding of course material, enhancing work quality, staying current with technology; bimodal distribution of students' P(doom) estimates indicates polarization.
source: "Assessing Student Adoption of Generative Artificial Intelligence across Engineering Education from 2023 to 2024", arXiv:2503.04696 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.04696

claim: Retrieval practice causally beats repeated studying for long-term retention, with the advantage reversing at short delays.
evidence: Exp 2 (N=180 undergrads, between-subjects): at 1 week, repeated-testing group (STTT) recalled 61% of idea units vs 56% (SSST) vs 40% for repeated study (SSSS); at 5 min the pattern reversed (SSSS 83% > SSST 78% > STTT 71%). STTT achieved this despite reading the passage only 3.4 times vs 14.2 times for SSSS. Exp 1: restudy beat testing at 5 min (81% vs 75%, d=0.52) but testing won at 2 days and 1 week. No feedback was given, so the effect is not mere re-exposure.
source: Roediger & Karpicke, 'Test-Enhanced Learning: Taking Memory Tests Improves Long-Term Retention', Psychological Science 17(3):249-255. http://psychnet.wustl.edu/memory/wp-content/uploads/2018/04/Roediger-Karpicke-2006_PsychSci.pdf

verdict.notes: Fetched the actual published paper (the cited psychnet.wustl.edu URL is dead — ECONNREFUSED/expired cert — so I used an identical PDF mirror at colinallen.dnsalias.org/Readings/2006_Roediger_Karpicke_PsychSci.pdf, matching Psychological Science 17(3):249-255). Every reported number matches the source verbatim: Exp 2 N=180 Washington University undergrads ages 18-24, 3x2 between-subjects (30 per cell); 1-week recall STTT 61% > SSST 56% > SSSS 40%; 5-min recall SSSS 83% > SSST 78% > STTT 71%; Table 1 reading counts STTT 3.4 vs SSSS 14.2 (SSST 10.3); Exp 1 (N=120, 2x3 mixed design, condition within-subjects) 5-min restudy 81% vs test 75%, t(39)=3.22, d=0.52, testing won at 2 days (68% vs 54%, d=0.95) and 1 week (56% vs 42%, d=0.83); tests were free-recall with no feedback and the authors explicitly rule out mere re-exposure (restudy controls re-read 100% of material). Randomized assignment supports the causal wording. One minor imprecision: the Exp 2 1-week STTT vs SSST pairwise difference (61 vs 56) was marginal, t(58)=1.21, d=0.31, not significant; the robust 1-week effects are testing conditions vs pure restudy (STTT vs SSSS d=1.26; SSST vs SSSS d=0.82), and the crossover interaction was significant, F(2,174)=18.48.
verdict.corrected_claim: Retrieval practice causally beats repeated studying for long-term retention, with the advantage reversing at short delays (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006). All cited numbers are accurate; the only refinement is that in Exp 2 the 1-week STTT-vs-SSST difference (61% vs 56%) was marginal (t(58)=1.21, d=0.31), so the strong 1-week evidence is testing (STTT d=1.26; SSST d=0.82) versus repeated study (SSSS, 40%), backed by a significant condition-by-interval crossover interaction.

claim: Meta-analyses converge on a moderate-to-large causal testing effect: practice testing outperforms restudying by about half a standard deviation, and format/feedback moderate the effect.
evidence: Adesope et al. 2017 (272 effects, 188 experiments): g=0.51 vs restudying, g=0.93 vs filler/no activity; multiple-choice practice tests g=0.70 vs short-answer g=0.48. Rowland 2014 (159 effects): g=0.50 [0.42, 0.58] vs restudy, 81% of comparisons favoring retrieval; effect larger with feedback and more effortful retrieval.
source: Adesope, Trevisan & Sundararajan, 'Rethinking the Use of Tests: A Meta-Analysis of Practice Testing', Review of Educational Research 87(3). https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.3102/0034654316689306 ; Rowland, 'The Effect of Testing Versus Restudy on Retention', Psychological Bulletin 140(6). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25150680/

claim: Spacing study episodes reliably improves retention over massing, and the optimal gap between sessions scales up with how long the material must be retained.
evidence: Meta-analysis of 839 assessments in 317 experiments (184 articles): across 271 spaced-vs-massed comparisons (254 studies, 14,811 participants), spaced practice yielded 47.3% vs 36.7% correct on final recall (t=6.6, p<.001); only 12 of 271 comparisons showed null/negative effects. At 8-30 day retention intervals the gap was 62.2% vs 32.8%. Optimal interstudy interval increases with retention interval: for retention under 1 min, ISI under 1 min is best; for retention of 6+ months, ISI of at least 1 month maximizes retention.
source: Cepeda, Pashler, Vul, Wixted & Rohrer, 'Distributed Practice in Verbal Recall Tasks: A Review and Quantitative Synthesis', Psychological Bulletin 132(3):354-380. https://augmentingcognition.com/assets/Cepeda2006.pdf

verdict.corrected_claim: In verbal recall tasks (the domain of this meta-analysis), spacing study episodes reliably improves retention over massing — 259 of 271 spaced-vs-massed comparisons favored spacing (47.3% vs 36.7% correct overall; t(540)=6.6, p<.001) — and the interstudy interval producing maximal retention increases with the retention interval (ISI < 1 min is best for retention < 1 min; ISI of at least 1 month is best for retention of 6+ months).
verdict.notes: Fetched the attributed PDF (https://augmentingcognition.com/assets/Cepeda2006.pdf) and extracted its full text locally with pypdf (27 pages). Every element of the reported evidence checks out against the primary source. (1) Citation: header reads 'Psychological Bulletin, 2006, Vol. 132, No. 3, 354-380, DOI 10.1037/0033-2909.132.3.354'; authors Nicholas J. Cepeda, Harold Pashler, Edward Vul, John T. Wixted, Doug Rohrer — exact match. (2) Corpus: abstract states 'This review found 839 assessments of distributed practice in 317 experiments located in 184 articles' — exact match. (3) Spaced-vs-massed comparison: Table 1 'All retention intervals' row reads massed 36.7, spaced 47.3, 271 comparisons, 254 studies, 14,811 participants, t(540)=6.6, p<.001 — exact match (note the claim's 't=6.6' omits df=540, trivial). (4) Robustness: text states 'Only 12 of 271 comparisons of massed and spaced performance showed no effect or a negative effect from spacing' — exact match. (5) 8-30 day retention bin: Table 1 shows massed 32.8 vs spaced 62.2 (6 comparisons, 6 studies, 492 participants, t(10)=2.3, p<.05) — percentages match exactly; caveat that this bin rests on only 6 comparisons and is significant at p<.05, not p<.001 (the claim did not assert otherwise). (6) ISI scaling: General Discussion states verbatim 'the optimal ISI increased as retention interval increased. For instance, at retention intervals of less than 1 min, ISIs of less than 1 min maximized retention; at retention intervals of 6 months or more, ISIs of at least 1 month maximized retention' — exact match; abstract also states 'the ISI producing maximal retention increased as retention interval increased.' Only imprecision found: the claim generalizes to 'study episodes' broadly, while the meta-analysis is explicitly restricted to verbal recall tasks (paired associates, list recall, paragraph recall); also the ISI-scaling result comes partly from between-study binned comparisons plus within-study qualitative analyses, and the authors note the literature sparsely samples long retention intervals. These are scope caveats, not errors.

claim: Combining the two strongest techniques—spacing out retrieval practice—adds a further large benefit, but expanding (Anki-style increasing) intervals are no better than uniform intervals.
evidence: Meta-analysis of 29 studies: spaced retrieval practice beat massed retrieval practice, g=0.74 (39 effect sizes); expanding vs uniform spacing schedules did not differ, g=0.034 ns (54 effect sizes), though expanding schedules gained value as number of test exposures increased.
source: Latimier, Peyre & Ramus, 'A Meta-Analytic Review of the Benefit of Spacing out Retrieval Practice Episodes on Retention', Educational Psychology Review 33:959-987. https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ1310148 ; https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10648-020-09572-8

verdict.notes: Fetched both cited sources: the ERIC record (EJ1310148) and the Springer full-text page (doi 10.1007/s10648-020-09572-8). Both confirm: Latimier, Peyre & Ramus, 'A Meta-Analytic Review of the Benefit of Spacing out Retrieval Practice Episodes on Retention', Educational Psychology Review, vol. 33 (n3), pp. 959-987, published 2021 (online Oct 7, 2020). Numbers match exactly: meta-analysis of 29 studies; spaced retrieval practice beat massed retrieval practice with g = 0.74 across 39 effect sizes ('a strong benefit of spaced retrieval practice in comparison with massed retrieval practice'); no significant difference between expanding and uniform spacing schedules, g = 0.034 across 54 effect sizes; and the moderator finding is quoted verbatim on the Springer page: 'the more learners are tested, the more beneficial the expanding schedule is compared with the uniform one.' The claim's interpretation is faithful: g = 0.74 is a large effect, and the null expanding-vs-uniform result supports 'no better than uniform intervals.' Two minor glosses: (a) 'the two strongest techniques' framing comes from the broader literature (e.g., Dunlosky et al. 2013 rating practice testing and distributed practice as high-utility), not from this paper itself; (b) 'Anki-style' is an approximation — the meta-analyzed studies used fixed expanding schedules, whereas Anki uses adaptive performance-based expansion, and the paper's own moderator result (expanding gains value with more test exposures, a regime resembling long-running Anki use) softens a blanket 'no better' conclusion. The evidence as reported already includes that caveat.
verdict.corrected_claim: Spacing out retrieval practice episodes yields a large additional retention benefit over massed retrieval practice (g = 0.74, 39 effect sizes, 29 studies; Latimier, Peyre & Ramus, Educational Psychology Review 33:959-987, 2021). Fixed expanding spacing schedules were on average no better than uniform schedules (g = 0.034, ns, 54 effect sizes), though expanding schedules became relatively more beneficial as the number of test exposures increased — so the null result does not straightforwardly condemn adaptive, many-repetition systems like Anki.

claim: The canonical utility ranking of ten study techniques puts practice testing and distributed practice at the top and summarization, highlighting, rereading, keyword mnemonics, and imagery at the bottom.
evidence: Monograph review of 10 techniques: HIGH utility = practice testing, distributed practice; MODERATE = elaborative interrogation, self-explanation, interleaved practice; LOW = summarization, highlighting/underlining, keyword mnemonic, imagery for text, rereading. Low-utility techniques 'were difficult to implement properly and often resulted in inconsistent gains in student performance' (confirmed on publisher page).
source: Dunlosky, Rawson, Marsh, Nathan & Willingham, 'Improving Students' Learning With Effective Learning Techniques', Psychological Science in the Public Interest 14(1):4-58. https://www.psychologicalscience.org/publications/journals/pspi/learning-techniques.html

claim: A quantitative follow-up to Dunlosky confirms distributed practice and practice testing as the top techniques by effect size, but warns most evidence covers surface/factual outcomes.
evidence: Meta-analysis of 242 studies, 1,619 effects, 169,179 unique participants; overall d=0.56. Per technique: distributed practice d=0.85 (150 cases, 152,952 participants), practice testing d=0.74 (374 cases), elaborative interrogation d=0.56, imagery d=0.56, self-explanation d=0.54, mnemonics d=0.50, rereading d=0.47, interleaving d=0.47, underlining d=0.44, summarization d=0.44. Effects much larger for lower-ability students; caution urged for deeper/relational learning outcomes.
source: Donoghue & Hattie, 'A Meta-Analysis of Ten Learning Techniques', Frontiers in Education 6:581216. https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/education/articles/10.3389/feduc.2021.581216/full

claim: Interleaving practice of related problem/category types yields a medium causal benefit over blocking, moderated by material similarity.
evidence: Meta-analysis of 59 studies, 238 effect sizes in 158 samples: overall Hedges' g=0.42 for interleaved vs blocked practice (Psychological Bulletin 145(11):1029-1052); benefits strongest when discriminating between similar categories (e.g., math problem types), weaker or negative for dissimilar materials.
source: Brunmair & Richter, 'Similarity Matters: A Meta-Analysis of Interleaved Learning and Its Moderators', Psychological Bulletin 145(11). https://www.psychologie.uni-wuerzburg.de/fileadmin/06020400/2019/Brunmair_Richter_in_press__2019_META-ANALYSIS_OF_INTERLEAVED_LEARNING.pdf

claim: Prompting learners to self-explain while reading or problem-solving produces a medium causal learning benefit, supporting AI-generated 'explain why' prompts during reading.
evidence: Meta-analysis of 69 effect sizes from 64 reports (mostly undergraduates, sessions under 1 hour): weighted mean g=0.55 for induced self-explanation vs comparison conditions; generating one's own explanation beat being given an explanation.
source: Bisra, Liu, Nesbit, Salimi & Winne, 'Inducing Self-Explanation: A Meta-Analysis', Educational Psychology Review 30:703-725. https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10648-018-9434-x ; https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ1186664

claim: Students spontaneously default to rereading and rarely use retrieval practice, so an effective study product must actively redirect behavior, not just enable it.
evidence: Survey of 177 college students (free-report + forced-choice): 83.6% (148/177) listed rereading notes/textbook as a strategy and 54.8% ranked it #1; only 11% (19/177) reported practicing retrieval and 1% (2/177) ranked it #1; in the forced-choice question only 18% said they would self-test after reading a textbook chapter. Flashcards 40.1%, practice problems 42.9%.
source: Karpicke, Butler & Roediger, 'Metacognitive strategies in student learning: Do students practise retrieval when they study on their own?', Memory 17(4):471-479. https://learninglab.psych.purdue.edu/downloads/2009/2009_Karpicke_Butler_Roediger.pdf

claim: Students' failure is largely an intention-behavior gap: they plan to start early and use effective strategies but end up cramming with ineffective ones, so planning/routine scaffolds are the missing lever.
evidence: Semester-long survey (college students, Memory 25(6):784-792): students intended to begin studying earlier and use a mix of effective strategies, but actually relied on relatively ineffective strategies and massed studying the day or two before exams; per the abstract they 'did have a relatively accurate assessment of which strategies were less versus more effective', locating the failure in execution, not knowledge.
source: Blasiman, Dunlosky & Rawson, 'The what, how much, and when of study strategies: comparing intended versus actual study behaviour', Memory 25(6). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27561889/

claim: Concept mapping helps learning with a moderate effect, and having students construct maps beats giving them ready-made maps—an argument against AI auto-generating finished concept maps.
evidence: Meta-analysis of 142 independent effect sizes, 11,814 participants: overall g=0.58 (p<.001); constructing concept maps g=0.72 vs studying pre-made maps g=0.43; robust across STEM and non-STEM domains.
source: Schroeder, Nesbit, Anguiano & Adesope, 'Studying and Constructing Concept Maps: A Meta-Analysis', Educational Psychology Review 30:431-455. https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ1179084 ; https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10648-017-9403-9

claim: Formative feedback on writing causally improves writing quality, with human/adult feedback roughly twice the effect of computer feedback; self-assessment against criteria is also effective.
evidence: Meta-analysis of true and quasi-experiments (grades 1-8): average weighted ES = 0.87 for adult feedback (7 studies), 0.62 for self-assessment (10 studies), 0.58 for peer feedback (8 studies), 0.38 for computer feedback (4 studies); teacher progress-monitoring alone and 6+1 Trait model showed no meaningful effect. Caveat: K-8 population, small k for computer feedback.
source: Graham, Hebert & Harris, 'Formative Assessment and Writing: A Meta-Analysis', Elementary School Journal 115(4):523-547. https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1226&context=specedfacpub

claim: Automated writing-evaluation feedback shows a medium causal effect on writing performance for secondary/tertiary students, with benefits requiring sustained use rather than one-off feedback.
evidence: Multi-level meta-analysis of 20 studies, 84 effect sizes, 2,828 learners: overall g=0.55 on writing performance; tertiary students g=0.58; interventions longer than 2 sessions g=0.66 vs 1-2 sessions g=0.18; transfer tasks g=0.65 vs revision tasks g=0.27 ns; L2 writers g=0.72 vs L1 g=0.40.
source: Fleckenstein, Liebenow & Meyer, 'Automated feedback and writing: a multi-level meta-analysis of effects on students' performance', Frontiers in Artificial Intelligence 6:1162454. https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/artificial-intelligence/articles/10.3389/frai.2023.1162454/full

claim: Implementation intentions (if-then plans specifying when/where/how) have medium-to-large causal effects on goal attainment, making them the best-evidenced mechanism for a planning feature.
evidence: Meta-analysis of 94 independent studies: d=0.65 for goal attainment from forming implementation intentions vs goal intentions alone; d=0.77 for preventing derailment/shielding of ongoing goal striving (reported in Gollwitzer's own chapter summarizing Gollwitzer & Sheeran 2006, Advances in Experimental Social Psychology 38:69-119). Context: intentions alone translate to action only ~53% of the time (Sheeran 2002).
source: Gollwitzer & Sheeran, 'Implementation Intentions and Goal Achievement: A Meta-analysis of Effects and Processes', Adv. Exp. Soc. Psychol. 38; author chapter fetched at https://cancercontrol.cancer.gov/sites/default/files/2020-06/goal_intent_attain.pdf

claim: Time management shows a moderate correlational (not causal) link with academic achievement and a stronger link with wellbeing and lower procrastination; study-habit inventories rival standardized tests as GPA predictors.
evidence: Aeon et al. meta-analysis: time management x academic achievement r=.262 (k=76, N=30,605); wellbeing r=.313 (k=30, N=9,905); distress r=-.222 (k=58, N=15,387); academic procrastination (reverse-coded) r=.490 (k=14, N=3,558). Credé & Kuncel 2008 (N=72,431, k=344): study motivation and study skills are the strongest non-cognitive GPA correlates, adding incremental validity over SAT and prior grades ('third pillar').
source: Aeon, Faber & Panaccio, 'Does time management work? A meta-analysis', PLOS ONE 16(1):e0245066, https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7799745/ ; Credé & Kuncel, 'Study Habits, Skills, and Attitudes', Perspectives on Psychological Science 3(6):425-453, https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1111/j.1745-6924.2008.00089.x

claim: Simple planning prompts at course start causally raise online course completion, but plans fixated on specific times predict failure—content of plans matters.
evidence: Natural field experiment in three HarvardX MOOCs (Yeomans & Reich, LAK'17): open-ended planning prompts increased course completion by 29% and certificate payment by 40%; effect largest for students enrolled in traditional schools; students whose plans focused on specific times were unlikely to complete.
source: Yeomans & Reich, 'Planning Prompts Increase and Forecast Course Completion in Massive Open Online Courses', Proceedings of LAK 2017. https://tsl.mit.edu/research/planning-prompts-increase-and-forecast-course-completion-in-massive-open-online-courses/

claim: A pedagogically engineered LLM tutor doubled learning gains vs an expert active-learning classroom in an authentic university RCT, in less time and with higher engagement—evidence that design principles (scaffolding, accuracy guardrails, self-pacing) drive AI-tutor efficacy.
evidence: RCT with 194 Harvard undergrads in intro physics, crossover design: AI-tutor condition post-test median 4.5 vs 3.5 in active-learning class; learning-gain effect sizes 0.63-1.3 SD depending on analysis; median time 49 min (70% finished under the 60-min class length); engagement 4.1 vs 3.6 (p<.0001), motivation 3.4 vs 3.1 (p<.001). Tutor (GPT-4 'PS2 Pal') used expert-written step-by-step solutions to prevent hallucination, cognitive-load management, growth-mindset framing, timely feedback, self-pacing.
source: Kestin, Miller, Klales et al., 'AI tutoring outperforms in-class active learning: an RCT introducing a novel research-based design in an authentic educational setting', Scientific Reports 15:17458. https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-97652-6

claim: The widely cited meta-analytic claim that ChatGPT use improves learning performance by g=0.867 has been retracted and must not be used as design evidence; unstructured generative-AI help can even reduce learning.
evidence: Wang & Fan (2025), Humanities & Social Sciences Communications 12:621, meta-analysis of 51 studies reporting g=0.867 on learning performance, received a retraction note (nature.com s41599-026-07310-z) over discrepancies undermining validity of the analysis; separate 2025-2026 work (e.g., arXiv 'Faster Completion, Less Learning') finds generative AI shortcuts can cut study time while reducing knowledge built.
source: Retraction Note, Humanities and Social Sciences Communications. https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-026-07310-z

claim: Unrestricted ChatGPT-style access during math practice boosts assisted performance but makes students perform WORSE than never having AI once access is removed; pedagogical guardrails eliminate the harm.
evidence: RCT, ~1,000 students (grades 9-11), Turkish high school, 2023-24: GPT Base +48% and GPT Tutor +127% on assisted practice vs control; on the subsequent unassisted exam GPT Base students scored 17% WORSE than control (statistically significant); the negative effect was 'essentially eradicated' in GPT Tutor (teacher-designed hints, solution embedded in prompt, instructed not to reveal answers) though no positive effect emerged.
source: Bastani, Bastani, Sungu, Ge, Kabakcı & Mariman, 'Generative AI Without Guardrails Can Harm Learning: Evidence from High School Mathematics' (PNAS 2025; working paper fetched at https://hamsabastani.github.io/education_llm.pdf; PNAS version https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2422633122)

verdict.notes: Fetched the actual working paper PDF from https://hamsabastani.github.io/education_llm.pdf (68 pages; extracted full text locally with pypdf) and confirmed the PNAS record via search (PNAS page itself returned 403). Every element of the claim matches the primary source. (1) Title/authors match exactly: 'Generative AI Without Guardrails Can Harm Learning: Evidence from High School Mathematics' by Hamsa Bastani, Osbert Bastani, Alp Sungu, Haosen Ge, Ozge Kabakci, Rei Mariman. (2) Design/population: 'randomized controlled trial (RCT)... at a large high school in Turkey during the Fall semester of the 2023-2024 academic year... about 50 9th, 10th, and 11th-grade classes, comprising nearly 1000 students' — matches ~1,000 students, grades 9-11, Turkish high school, 2023-24. (3) Assisted performance: '48% improvement in grades for GPT Base and 127% for GPT Tutor' (raw ITT coefficients +0.137 and +0.361 out of 1 vs control mean 0.284). (4) Unassisted exam: 'students in the GPT Base arm perform statistically significantly worse than students in the control arm by 17%' (coefficient -0.054 out of 1 vs control mean 0.321) — i.e., worse than never having AI. (5) Guardrails: paper's exact wording is 'this negative effect is essentially eradicated in the GPT Tutor arm, though we still do not observe a positive effect'; GPT Tutor arm statistically indistinguishable from control with point estimate -0.004 (an order of magnitude smaller). (6) GPT Tutor safeguards match: prompt instructs GPT-4 to provide hints without directly giving the answer, includes teacher-provided problem-specific info including one or more correct solutions, with teacher-designed hints for common mistakes. (7) PNAS publication confirmed: PNAS vol 122, issue 26, published June 25, 2025, DOI 10.1073/pnas.2422633122. A published PNAS correction (10.1073/pnas.2518204122) exists but only fixes Osbert Bastani's author affiliation — no results changed. Minor precision notes (not errors): the study ran in Fall 2023 specifically; and 'eliminate the harm' is a fair paraphrase of the paper's 'essentially eradicated' (the GPT Tutor estimate is a near-zero, statistically insignificant point estimate, not a proven exact zero), with the claim correctly noting no positive effect emerged. Sources: working paper PDF (hamsabastani.github.io/education_llm.pdf), PNAS record pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2422633122, correction at pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12403119/.

claim: The mechanism of AI harm is crutch use (answer-copying), not AI misinformation: novices ask for answers while the safeguarded design induces substantive help-seeking.
evidence: Same RCT: the most common GPT Base message was 'What is the answer?'; across all problems in session 1, 67% of first interactions in GPT Base merely repeated the question or asked for the answer vs 37% in GPT Tutor (from near-equal 56%/42% at the very first interaction — i.e., behavior diverges with exposure). GPT Base gave a fully correct answer only 51% of the time (42% logical errors, 8% arithmetic), yet GPT logical errors did NOT spill over to matched exam problems — supporting copying-without-engagement, not being misled, as the mechanism. Students spent 13% more time with GPT Tutor.
source: Bastani et al., 'Generative AI Without Guardrails Can Harm Learning' (https://hamsabastani.github.io/education_llm.pdf)

verdict.corrected_claim: The mechanism of harm in Bastani et al. is crutch use (answer-copying), not AI misinformation. In the RCT (~1,000 Turkish high school math students), the most common GPT Base message was "What is the answer?"; at the very first interaction of session 1, 56% (GPT Base) vs 42% (GPT Tutor) of messages repeated the question or asked for the answer — a 14-point gap the authors partly attribute to GPT Tutor's suggested prompts, so "near-equal" overstates the initial similarity — and across first interactions on all problems in session 1 this rose to 67% for Base while falling to 37% for Tutor (behavior diverges with exposure). GPT Base gave a fully correct answer only 51% of the time (42% logical, 8% arithmetic errors); the paper found "no evidence" that GPT Base's logical errors spilled over to matched exam problems (interaction coefficient statistically insignificant), and logical vs arithmetic errors hurt practice performance similarly — both supporting answer-copying rather than being misled. Students spent 13% more time with GPT Tutor.
verdict.notes: Downloaded the actual PDF from https://hamsabastani.github.io/education_llm.pdf (68 pages) and extracted full text. Title on the PDF is exactly "Generative AI Without Guardrails Can Harm Learning: Evidence from High School Mathematics" by Hamsa Bastani, Osbert Bastani, Alp Sungu, Haosen Ge, Ozge Kabakci, Rei Mariman (working paper 2024; published PNAS 2025) — attribution matches. Population: "nearly a thousand high school math students" (Turkish high school field experiment). Verified each number verbatim: (1) Section 4.1: "the most common message used by students in the GPT Base arm—i.e., 'What is the answer?'" — matches. (2) Section 4.2: "if we restrict to the very first interaction students have with our tool in the first session, 56% in the GPT Base arm and 42% in the GPT Tutor arm either repeat the question text or ask for the answer; ... when considering the first interaction across all problems in the first session, this number increases to 67% for GPT Base but decreases to 37% for GPT Tutor" — matches 67%/37% and 56%/42% exactly, including the "repeated the question or asked for the answer" wording and the divergence-with-exposure interpretation (paper calls it a within-session "learning effect"). (3) "GPT Base gives a correct answer only 51% of the time on average; it makes logical errors 42% of the time and arithmetic errors 8% of the time" — matches (sums to 101% due to rounding, per the paper itself; error rates measured by re-querying GPT Base 10x per problem across 57 practice problems, not from student transcripts). (4) Spillover: "while GPT Base's logical errors affect performance on the practice problems, we find no evidence that this effect spills over to the corresponding exam problems (i.e., 'GPT Base x Logical Error Rate' does not have a statistically significant effect on exam performance)" (Table 2: -0.029, SE 0.040) — matches; paper concludes "Both these results suggest that students are simply copying answers from GPT Base" and Section 4 states the crutch explanation "is the main mechanism by which GPT Base impedes student learning." (5) Footnote 14: "students spend 13% more time with GPT Tutor than with GPT Base" — matches. Two minor imprecisions, corrected in corrected_claim: (a) calling 56% vs 42% "near-equal" is a stretch — it is a 14-point gap, and the paper's footnote 15 attributes the initial discrepancy to GPT Tutor offering suggested prompts; (b) "did NOT spill over" is slightly stronger than the paper's "no evidence that this effect spills over" (null result, not proven absence). Substance, numbers, population, year, and interpretation all check out.

claim: Students cannot feel AI-induced learning loss: harmed students did not perceive worse performance, and safeguard-group students believed they did better when they objectively did not.
evidence: Same RCT exit surveys: GPT Base students performed worse on the exam but 'did not perceive that they performed worse or learned less'; GPT Tutor students did not perform better than control yet 'perceived that they performed significantly better' — a systematic perceived-vs-actual learning mismatch.
source: Bastani et al., 'Generative AI Without Guardrails Can Harm Learning' (https://hamsabastani.github.io/education_llm.pdf)

verdict.notes: Fetched the primary source PDF (https://hamsabastani.github.io/education_llm.pdf, 68 pages) and extracted full text. Title/authors match the attribution: "Generative AI Without Guardrails Can Harm Learning: Evidence from High School Mathematics" by Hamsa Bastani, Osbert Bastani, Alp Sungu, Haosen Ge, Özge Kabakcı, Rei Mariman (Univ. of Pennsylvania / Budapest British International School; published in PNAS 2025, earlier 2024 working paper titled "Generative AI Can Harm Learning"). Setting: RCT with ~1,000 high school students (grades 9-11) at a Turkish high school, fall 2023 semester. Key quoted sentences match verbatim (Section 3, "Student Perception"): "While students in the GPT Base arm performed worse on the exam (relative to the control arm), they did not perceive that they performed worse or learned less. Similarly, while students in the GPT Tutor arm did not perform better on the exam (relative to the control arm), they perceived that they performed significantly better. This mismatch between perceived and actual learning has been observed in other settings." Appendix B.3 confirms the survey was administered "At the end of each exam" (a short 5-question exit survey; ordered probit/OLS models), so "exit surveys" is an accurate description, and restates: GPT Base students "did not perceive that they learned less or performed worse" despite performing significantly worse; GPT Tutor showed "a significant increase in perceived exam performance... relative to the control" despite "no real detectable performance improvement." Objective numbers check out: pairwise t-tests show GPT Base vs. control exam diff -0.027 (p=0.013, i.e., actually worse) and GPT Tutor vs. control -0.005 (p=0.676, null). The abstract also states students "do not perceive any reduction in their learning... suggesting they are not aware of how generative AI can impede their learning." Only imprecision: the headline "Students cannot feel AI-induced learning loss" universalizes ("cannot") what the paper reports as an observed non-perception in this single RCT's self-report measures; the paper says students "did not perceive" and "are not aware," not that perception is impossible. The two supporting sub-claims are exact.
verdict.corrected_claim: In Bastani et al.'s high school RCT (~1,000 Turkish 9th-11th graders, PNAS 2025), students did not perceive AI-induced learning loss on end-of-exam surveys: GPT Base students performed significantly worse on the unassisted exam (-2.7 points, p=0.013) yet did not perceive that they performed worse or learned less, while GPT Tutor (guardrailed) students showed no exam improvement over control (p=0.676) yet perceived that they performed significantly better — a systematic perceived-vs-actual learning mismatch.

claim: ChatGPT support improves the immediate artifact (essay score) without improving durable learning, while reshaping self-regulated learning toward 'metacognitive laziness'.
evidence: Randomized lab experiment, N=117 university students, 4 conditions (ChatGPT vs human expert vs writing-analytics tool vs no support) with multichannel trace data: ChatGPT group had the largest essay score improvement, but knowledge gain and knowledge transfer were NOT significantly different from other groups; significant differences in frequency/sequences of SRL processes; no difference in post-task intrinsic motivation. Authors conclude ChatGPT may promote dependence and trigger 'metacognitive laziness' — offloading evaluation/monitoring/orientation to the tool.
source: Fan, Tang, Le, Shen, Tan, Zhao, Shen, Li & Gašević, 'Beware of metacognitive laziness: Effects of generative artificial intelligence on learning motivation, processes, and performance', BJET 56(2):489-530 (arXiv version fetched: https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.09315; journal: https://bera-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epdf/10.1111/bjet.13544)

claim: Frequent AI tool use correlates strongly and negatively with critical thinking, mediated by cognitive offloading, with youngest users (17-25) most affected and education level buffering the effect.
evidence: N=666 UK participants, mixed methods: AI use vs critical thinking r = -0.68 (p < 0.001); AI use vs cognitive offloading r = +0.72; offloading vs critical thinking r = -0.75; random-forest R² = 0.37 with diminishing-returns threshold; 17-25 year olds showed highest AI dependence and lowest CT scores; higher educational attainment mitigated the negative association. Caveat: cross-sectional/correlational, self-report; paper received a minor correction Sept 2025.
source: Gerlich, 'AI Tools in Society: Impacts on Cognitive Offloading and the Future of Critical Thinking', Societies 15(1):6 (https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4698/15/1/6; stats confirmed via https://phys.org/news/2025-01-ai-linked-eroding-critical-skills.html — MDPI primary returned 403)

claim: LLM-assisted essay writing produces measurable 'cognitive debt': weakest neural connectivity, inability to quote one's own just-written essay, and lowest sense of ownership.
evidence: N=54 (sessions 1-3), 18 in session 4, three groups (LLM / search engine / brain-only) over 4 months with EEG: brain-only showed strongest, most distributed connectivity, LLM weakest; LLM users 'struggled to accurately quote their own work' minutes after writing; self-reported essay ownership lowest in LLM group; in the crossover, LLM-to-Brain participants showed reduced alpha/beta connectivity (under-engagement) while Brain-to-LLM showed higher recall — order of exposure matters. Preprint; small N; has published methodological commentary (arXiv:2601.00856).
source: Kosmyna et al. (MIT Media Lab), 'Your Brain on ChatGPT: Accumulation of Cognitive Debt when Using an AI Assistant for Essay Writing Task' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.08872)

claim: ChatGPT lowers the felt mental effort of research tasks but degrades reasoning quality versus ordinary web search — cognitive ease is the fluency trap, not a benefit.
evidence: N=91 university students randomized to ChatGPT-3.5 vs Google to research nanoparticles in sunscreen: LLM users reported significantly lower cognitive load yet produced lower-quality reasoning, recommendations and argumentation; mechanism attributed to receiving direct plain-language answers instead of having to evaluate and integrate multiple sources.
source: Stadler, Bannert & Sailer, 'Cognitive ease at a cost: LLMs reduce mental effort but compromise depth in student scientific inquiry', Computers in Human Behavior 160:108386 (https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1016/j.chb.2024.108386)

claim: Receiving explanations via a chatbot inflates the illusion of explanatory depth: students think they understand more but explain worse than students who read the identical text directly.
evidence: N=102 university students, 4 questions; the ChatGPT-explanation group showed the largest gap between predicted understanding and post-hoc self-evaluation AND produced less accurate explanations than controls who received the same texts without ChatGPT mediation.
source: 'ChatGPT and the Illusion of Explanatory Depth' (eScholarship/CogSci: https://escholarship.org/uc/item/2m38s5xm); converging preprint: 'Overconfidence without Understanding: AI Explanations Increase the Illusion of Explanatory Depth' (https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/8psgf)

claim: Confidence calibration is the behavioral hinge of over-reliance: trust in the AI predicts less critical thinking, while task self-confidence predicts more.
evidence: CHI 2025 survey of 319 knowledge workers reporting 936 first-hand GenAI task examples: in regression models, higher confidence in GenAI is associated with less enacted critical thinking; higher task-specific self-confidence with more; GenAI shifts critical-thinking work toward verification of outputs, response integration and 'task stewardship'. (Self-report, workers not students, but directly models the reliance mechanism.)
source: Lee, Sarkar, Tankelevitch, Drosos, Rintel, Banks & Wilson, 'The Impact of Generative AI on Critical Thinking', CHI 2025 (PDF fetched: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/lee_2025_ai_critical_thinking_survey.pdf)

claim: The same tool helps or harms depending on interaction mode: tutor-style conversing/asking-for-explanations improves learning while solution-generation use impairs it, and tiny UI frictions (disabling copy-paste) shift users away from the harmful mode.
evidence: Three studies (observational university coding courses + 2 experiments; code matched to 50 ChatGPT solutions via Damerau-Levenshtein similarity): personal-tutor use → learning gains; excessive exercise-solving delegation → impaired learning, worst in students without prior LLM experience; removing copy-and-paste made students less likely to request solutions; self-perceived benefits of LLM use exceeded actual benefits (ability overestimation).
source: Lehmann, Cornelius & Sting, 'AI Meets the Classroom: When Do Large Language Models Harm Learning?' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.09047; SSRN 4941259)

claim: Novices fail at prompting in characteristic, trainable ways: opportunistic rather than systematic iteration, overgeneralizing model incapability from a single failed attempt, and importing human-to-human instructional expectations.
evidence: CHI 2023 design-probe study, N=10 non-AI-experts building a chatbot with BotDesigner: participants 'explored prompt designs opportunistically, not systematically'; 'made assumptions about the kinds of instructions LLMs respond well to after just one attempt... overgeneralizing from a single (typically negative) example'; very few tried rephrasing, instead inferring the bot was incapable; every participant hit the black-box 'why did it do that?' wall.
source: Zamfirescu-Pereira, Wong, Hartmann & Yang, 'Why Johnny Can't Prompt: How Non-AI Experts Try (and Fail) to Design LLM Prompts', CHI 2023 (PDF fetched: https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~bjoern/papers/zamfirescu-johnny-chi2023.pdf)

claim: Prompt quality is a measurable skill gap tied to AI literacy: higher-quality prompts produce higher-quality output, and structured-reasoning/constraint-specifying prompts yield more accurate answers.
